entry.title: Combined summary - Fly Client Proposal
entry.summary: The FlyClient protocol has sparked some interest and debate among developers in the Bitcoin community. Tom Harding introduced the concept, highlighting its potential to allow light clients to verify the work of the heaviest chain with a roughly 100KB proof. This could be implemented as a soft fork by inserting a single hash into the coinbase transaction, similar to the method used for SegWit commitments. The detailed discussion can be found in a linked video presentation [here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BPNs9EVxWrA&t=8386s). Nuh.dev pointed out that FlyClient, while functioning similarly to standard light clients regarding trust assumptions, offers enhanced efficiency. Unlike traditional light clients which need to download all block headers, FlyClient only requires a constant, small number of headers. This makes it more efficient, especially given the trust placed in the merkle root once it is sufficiently confirmed by proof of work. Zac Mitton compared FlyClient to SPV clients, noting its utility, particularly for blockchains with higher daily header outputs than Bitcoin. He mentioned an alternative technology, STARK proofs, which do not require soft forks for implementation and are described in detail [here](https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/raito). Mitton suggested that efforts might be better spent on technologies like STARK proofs, especially considering the challenges of achieving consensus for new soft forks as Bitcoin becomes more rigid in its structure. Further discussions touched upon the security and simplicity of FlyClient compared to STARK proofs. Nuh.dev emphasized FlyClient's lack of new cryptographic assumptions beyond those already utilized in Bitcoin, such as the "honest mining majority." In contrast, STARK proofs, while post-quantum secure, involve more complex elements like polynomials and hash functions but do not depend on the trust assumptions required by other knowledge proofs like SNARKs. Lastly, there was a brief mention of an alternative approach discussed by Benedict Bünz, where an SPV client polling the network could potentially handle tasks traditionally managed by input inclusion proofs without necessitating any forking changes. This highlights ongoing discussions around optimizing light client protocols and their implementations within the existing framework of Bitcoin.
